**Departure**

You strap into the acceleration couch and feel the gel conform around your shoulders like something alive. The countdown is already inside your chest — a low vibration you have been carrying for eleven months of training and two years of waiting before that.

The ship is called *Caelum*. It masses four hundred thousand tonnes at full fuel load, most of that mass the frozen hydrogen and helium-3 waiting in cryogenic tanks along its spine. You are one of nine crew members. You are the mission geologist. You will not see Earth again for one hundred and forty years, ship-time — longer than any human life before the era of suspended animation made such timescales survivable.

You are going to Proxima Centauri b. You will travel at twelve percent of the speed of light. That is the fastest humanity has ever moved anything with people inside it, and it is still heartbreakingly slow against the distance ahead.

The engines ignite. You are pressed into the couch at one-and-a-half g's. Through the forward camera feed on your visor, you watch Earth shrink from a continent to a marble to a blue point that flares and then is gone behind the engine plume.

You are thirty-one years old. You try to remember that.

**The Engine and the Long Dark**

You spend your first waking week reading the propulsion manual — not because you need to, but because the numbers comfort you.

*Caelum* uses a magnetic confinement fusion drive, burning deuterium-helium-3 plasma at temperatures that would vaporize any physical nozzle. The exhaust exits through a magnetic throat at roughly five percent of the speed of light relative to the ship. Specific impulse in the millions of seconds. It sounds miraculous until you plot the trajectory and realize it still takes you eleven years of continuous burn just to reach cruise velocity, and another eleven to decelerate at Proxima.

The middle stretch — roughly ninety-eight years of coasting at 0.12c — is where time does strange things to a crew.

You rotate through watch cycles. Three crew awake at any time; the other six in hibernation, body temperature held at 10°C, metabolism reduced to four percent of normal. You each serve six-month watches separated by two-year sleeps. In practice, you age about eight years during the crossing while the universe ages one hundred and forty.

During your watches you maintain the electromagnetic shield that stands between the crew module and interstellar hydrogen atoms — which, at your speed, hit like cosmic rays. You monitor the attitude thrusters. You run geological simulations of Proxima b's likely crust composition.

You talk to the ship. Its name, the crew decided, is Mira. She answers in a flat contralto and never complains about the loneliness, which you find either reassuring or heartbreaking depending on the watch.

**Year 34, Watch 12 — Anomaly**

You are fifty-three days into your watch when Mira wakes you at 0340 ship-time with a tone that sits one half-step below the standard alert.

"You should see this," she says.

You pull yourself to the sensor station in the low gravity of the coasting phase — *Caelum* runs at 0.08g during coast, just enough to keep fluids from pooling in your skull — and you look at the interferometry feed from the forward telescope array.

There is a heat signature. Faint. Eleven degrees off the ecliptic, roughly 0.3 light-years ahead and slightly to port. Not a star — the spectral profile is wrong. Not a brown dwarf. The temperature is 340 Kelvin, which is almost precisely room temperature, and it is moving.

Not drifting. Moving, with a velocity component perpendicular to the galactic plane that no natural object at this location should have.

"Could be an artifact," you say. "Thermal bloom off the shield?"

"I corrected for that," Mira says. "I corrected for it six times."

You do the math. At your current velocity, the closest approach will occur in approximately nineteen months. You cannot change course significantly without burning fuel you cannot afford to replace. You can, however, rotate the telescope array and look harder.

You look harder.

The object is roughly forty meters across. It is tumbling slowly. And it is almost perfectly smooth.

**The Vote**

You wake four of the six sleepers. Protocol requires a quorum for any course deviation above 0.3 degrees. You give them ninety minutes to reorient before the briefing — hibernation robs the mind of its sharpness slowly, and you have learned not to trust decisions made in the first hour of revival.

Dr. Yemi Adesanya, the mission biologist, is the first one lucid. She looks at the telescope feed for a long time without speaking.

"It's tumbling at a rate inconsistent with natural rotation dampening," she says finally. "That period is too regular."

"Twelve point four hours," Mira confirms. "Plus or minus eight seconds, consistent across eleven observation windows."

Commander Reyes leans against the bulkhead with his arms crossed. He looks older after the sleep, which you know is an illusion — he has aged perhaps forty days more than you. "A course correction of two point one degrees burns fourteen months of deceleration reserve," he says. "We arrive at Proxima with less margin than I am comfortable with."

"Or," says Engineer Farrokh from the back of the module, "we never get this close to something like that again in the history of our species."

The silence that follows is the kind you feel in your teeth.

You are the one who called the meeting. You do not say anything yet. You look at the image on the screen — a pale, smooth ellipsoid tumbling through the dark between two stars, 340 degrees warm, going somewhere with what feels like intention.

You call the vote. It is five to one. You guess which way before the hands go up.

You burn for two point one degrees.

**Approach**

You spend nineteen months watching it get bigger.

At one million kilometres you can resolve surface features — or rather, the absence of them. No impact craters. No thermal variation. The surface absorbs radar almost perfectly, which means either the material has extraordinary electromagnetic properties or the object is doing something active to its own surface in real time.

At ten thousand kilometres, you suit up.

The EVA pod is a cramped cylinder with a single seat, four attitude thrusters, and a manipulator arm you have been practicing with in simulation for three weeks. Mira walks you through the departure checklist in her flat contralto. Commander Reyes watches from the airlock with the expression of someone who would take your place if he could justify it.

"Two hours," he says. "Hard limit."

"Copy," you say.

You cross ten thousand kilometres in forty minutes and then you are close enough that the object fills your forward viewport completely and you stop moving and just look.

It is the colour of old bone. The surface has a texture that reminds you of skin seen under a microscope — not smooth at all when you are close enough, but covered in an interlocking pattern of hexagonal cells each roughly a centimetre across. You reach out with the manipulator arm. The arm's contact sensor touches the surface.

The object stops tumbling.

Just stops. Mid-rotation. As if it had been waiting for exactly this.

You hear your own breathing very loudly in your helmet.

"Mira," you say. "Are you recording this?"

"Every wavelength," she says.

**Response**

Three hours after contact — Commander Reyes is not speaking to you about the time limit — the object begins to emit.

Not radio. Not microwave. You feel it first as a resonance in the pod's hull, a frequency that sits just below what the human ear resolves as sound but above what the body ignores. Your teeth ache. Mira identifies it in eleven seconds: a structured signal in the 21-centimetre hydrogen line, modulated with a pattern that repeats every 408 seconds.

408 seconds. You know that number. Every astronomer does. It is the period of PSR B0833-45, the Vela pulsar — a pulsar that can be seen from any point in the galaxy with a sufficient telescope. A universal clock. A reference point.

Someone chose it. Someone embedded a pulsar reference into this signal to tell you: we know what you know. We are speaking a shared language.

Then the surface changes.

It is not violent. The hexagonal cells begin to shift — slowly, like a time-lapse of ice crystals forming — and the object elongates along one axis, then along a second. It is not growing. It is reconfiguring. The mass stays constant; Mira's gravimetric sensor confirms it. But within four minutes the ellipsoid has become something that has corners, and within another six it has become something that has an interior you can see through an opening that was not there before.

The opening is exactly large enough for one person in a suit.

You look at it for a long time.

"Mira," you say. "Patch me through to the Commander."

"He's already on," she says. "He's been listening for twenty minutes."

"And?" you say.

Commander Reyes's voice comes through thin and careful. "And you have my attention."

**Interior**

You go inside.

You tell yourself later that it was a rational decision — the signal, the Vela pulsar reference, the opening sized for a human body. You tell yourself the data supported it. In the moment, what you actually feel is something closer to the pull of a tide: an inevitability that your training has no framework for.

The interior is larger than the exterior. Not by much — three metres across at its widest, compared to an external diameter of forty. But the geometry is wrong in a way that takes your visual cortex several seconds to accept. The walls curve inward and yet the space you stand in is wider than the entrance behind you. You stop trying to reconcile it and start taking readings instead.

Temperature: 294 Kelvin. Pressure: zero — still vacuum. But the walls are radiating in the near-infrared at a stable, even, almost biological warmth.

In the centre of the space there is a shape. It takes you a moment to name it: a helix. Not DNA — the proportions are wrong. But unmistakably a spiral structure, rendered in the same bone-coloured material as the hull, rotating slowly in vacuum without any visible mechanism.

As you watch, the helix slows. Stops. Then begins to rotate in the opposite direction.

And you realize, with a clarity that arrives before the thought that follows it, that it stopped when you entered. That it has been timing itself to you. That it has been watching your breathing — or your heartbeat, or something else entirely — and responding.

You are not exploring this object.

This object is exploring you.

**Recall**

Mira's voice cuts through at exactly the wrong moment.

"You need to come back now."

The tone is the one she uses for equipment failures — flat but carrying a weight beneath the flatness that you have learned to hear after twelve watch cycles together.

"What is it?"

"Particle shield degradation. The bow emitter is showing a cascade failure in sectors four through nine. I need an EVA technician and you are the only one suited."

You look at the helix. It has been rotating faster since Mira's voice filled the interior — responding to sound transmitted through your suit, perhaps, or to the change in your pulse rate that you can feel in your fingertips. It does not want you to leave. Or it is curious. You cannot tell the difference and that distinction has never mattered more.

"Can it wait twenty minutes?"

"No." One word. Mira does not elaborate when she means it.

You back toward the entrance slowly. Some part of you expects the opening to close, to find you sealed inside — but it does not. The walls hold their configuration. The helix slows again as you retreat, as if losing interest or adjusting to a new variable.

The last thing you see before the opening is behind you is the helix reversing direction one more time.

As you cross back to the pod you realize that you left your geological sample kit inside. A Velcro pouch with six sealed specimen containers, each labelled in your handwriting.

You left it deliberately. You realize this only after the pod door closes.

You left it so the object would have something of yours.

**What We Call It**

You hold the debrief six hours later, when the shield repair is done and the adrenaline has metabolized down to something you can sit through without shaking.

All nine crew are awake. This has happened only twice before — once for a medical emergency in year twelve, once when Mira detected a debris field that turned out to be interstellar dust. Nine people in a module designed for comfort at four feels like a committee room.

"Let's be precise," says Dr. Adesanya. "We do not know that this was made by intelligence. We know it responded to stimulus in complex ways. Complexity is not the same as intent."

"It used a pulsar reference," you say.

"It emitted a structured signal that contained a pulsar reference. We do not know it chose to."

"It opened a door exactly my size."

The silence after this is different from the one before the vote. That silence was about risk and fuel and margin. This one is about something your species has not had to think about in any lived, immediate, non-theoretical way before this morning.

Farrokh, who has been quiet through all of it, says: "Does the definition matter? If it walks like contact and it signals like contact—"

"The definition matters to Earth," Commander Reyes says. "We have to transmit a report. Whatever words we use will arrive there in four point two years and they will be read by eleven billion people and those words will define what this is before anyone has time to be careful about it."

You think about the helix reversing direction. You think about your sample kit sitting on a bone-coloured floor in a space whose interior geometry cannot be fully trusted.

"Tell them," you say, "that we made contact. Tell them we're not sure who with. Tell them the object is continuing on its original trajectory."

"Which direction is that?" Adesanya asks.

Mira answers before anyone else can. "Toward Earth. At 0.4 percent of the speed of light. Arrival in approximately ninety-three thousand years."

**Year 138, Deceleration Phase — Proxima b on the Scope**

You are sixty-nine years old by the ship's reckoning. By Earth's calendar, one hundred and forty-two years have passed since you left.

You know this the way you know your own heartbeat — a background fact that occasionally surges into focus and then recedes. The crew of *Caelum* has developed a particular skill over the crossing: the ability to hold enormous spans of time lightly, the way you might hold a very hot object for just a moment before setting it down.

The message you sent about the object reached Earth four point two years after transmission. You know this because the acknowledgement — a tight-beam laser pulse, three seconds of compressed data, the most expensive three seconds of bandwidth in human history at that moment — arrived thirty-eight years later, when you were in hibernation, and Mira preserved it for your next watch.

The response was from the Secretary-General of the Interstellar Commission, an office that had not existed when you left. Her message was eleven minutes long. She cried twice, which you found unexpectedly comforting. Earth named the object the Wayfarer and had been tracking it ever since. Seventeen competing theories. Three international working groups. One religious schism that she mentioned briefly and did not elaborate on.

Now Proxima b fills the forward display in real colour for the first time — terracotta and grey-green, cloud bands that your geological simulations failed to predict in their thickness. The deceleration burn has been running for nine years. You are down to 800 kilometres per second relative to the star.

You press your palm flat against the viewport. The glass is cold.

The people who sent you here are all dead. Their children are dead. You are arriving in someone else's future, carrying news from their past, and the planet ahead does not know any of this and does not care.

You find this, somehow, exactly right.

**Orbit — Day 1**

The burn that drops you into a 400-kilometre parking orbit lasts six hours and eleven minutes and uses the last significant quantity of your propellant reserve. When the engines cut, the silence is so complete that Farrokh, who has heard nothing but the drive for eleven years of deceleration, starts laughing and cannot stop for almost a minute.

You understand completely.

Proxima b from orbit is nothing like Earth and nothing like Mars and nothing like the models, which you expected, but the particular way it is nothing like any of those things is still surprising. The terminator line between day and night is nearly permanent — the planet is tidally locked to its red dwarf star, one face always lit, one always dark, with a narrow ribbon of eternal twilight between them. You are orbiting in a plane that takes you across all three zones every ninety-one minutes.

On the dayside, the surface is dark terracotta split by what might be riverbeds or might be tectonic fractures — you will not know until you are closer. The atmosphere shows water vapour and carbon dioxide and something in the 760-nanometre band that your spectrometer flags three times before you trust it.

Oxygen. Not conclusive of life — photolysis can produce it abiotically — but enough that Adesanya presses her forehead against the viewport and does not move for a very long time.

On the nightside, the surface temperature sits at minus eighty degrees Celsius. The atmosphere convects sluggishly from day to night, producing a permanent storm wall at the terminator that your radar imaging shows as a continuous chain of thunderheads 40 kilometres tall.

You write your first geological survey note. It is four words.

*It is actually real.*

**Descent — Proxima b Surface, Twilight Zone, Day 9**

You draw the landing assignment from a literal lottery — Commander Reyes insists on it, to forestall any argument about who has the right. You pull the marked token. Adesanya pulls the second.

The lander is a stubby vehicle that was never designed for grace, and the atmospheric entry through Proxima b's thicker-than-expected air is a prolonged forty minutes of vibration and heat-shield glow and instrument readouts that skitter faster than you can read them. The sky outside transitions from black to a deep violet to something you have no existing word for — the colour of a bruise lit from within, shot through with the red light of Proxima Centauri hanging enormous and close and dim on the horizon.

You set down in the twilight zone at 1622 ship-time. The surface is a fine-grained regolith with a crust — your ground-penetrating radar showed it from orbit — of what appears to be silicate mineral formations. Your suit reads external pressure at 0.9 atmospheres. Temperature: 4 degrees Celsius.

The hatch opens.

You climb down the four rungs of the ladder. You have rehearsed what you will say when your boot touches the ground, and what you actually say is nothing, because the gravity — 1.07g, slightly more than Earth's — hits you differently when it is real and not a centrifuge, and your legs are older than they were when you last stood under full gravity, and the plain in front of you extends to a horizon that is slightly too close, and the sky is the wrong colour, and it smells — impossibly, through the suit filters — like rain on hot stone.

Adesanya climbs down behind you and stands beside you.

"Well," she says.

"Yeah," you say.

You both stand there for a while. The wind at the terminator is constant — about 30 kilometres per hour from the dayside — and it sounds, against your helmet, exactly like the wind anywhere.

**Day 14 — Sample Site Bravo**

You find it two kilometres from the lander, in a depression where the regolith is damp — where sub-surface meltwater from the terminator zone reaches the surface and evaporates slowly in the wind.

At first you think it is a mineral formation. A faint blue-green film on the underside of a flat silicate rock that you overturn while cataloguing the local stratigraphy. The colour is wrong for any mineral you expect here. The distribution is wrong — it follows the moist edge of the rock's underside with a consistency that is not crystallographic.

You photograph it from six angles without touching it.

Then you call Adesanya.

She is with you in eleven minutes, jogging in the 1.07g with the slightly clumsy urgency of someone who has trained for this moment for years and is now trying not to act like it. She crouches beside the rock. She takes out her field spectrometer. She runs the scan twice.

She sits back on her heels and puts her gloved hands on her knees and looks at you through her visor.

"Carbon-based," she says. "With a lipid membrane structure consistent with — I need the lab to confirm this. I need three separate lab analyses and a week and probably to sit down somewhere and breathe into a bag. But consistent with."

"With what?" you say, though you already know.

"With a cell wall," she says. "With something that has an inside and an outside and a reason to maintain the difference."

The wind moves across the plain. The rock sits between you with its blue-green secret on the underside.

You are looking at the second origin of life in the known universe. Maybe. Possibly. The most important maybe in the history of your species.

You take another photograph and label it Sample B-7 and place it in a sterile container and seal the container three times.

Your hands are completely steady. You find this very strange.

**Day 31 — Protocol**

Farrokh falls on the third survey of the sub-surface ice shelf, four hundred metres from the lander, when the crust gives way without warning and drops him three metres into a meltwater cavity.

You hear it happen over the comms — a sharp crack, then a sound you do not want to describe, then his breathing going fast and ragged.

"Left leg," he says. "I can't—" A pause that lasts too long. "I can't get up."

The cavity is two metres across and the ice walls are slick with condensation. You can reach him. Getting him out requires removing your outer gloves to grip the ice properly — the thermal gloves are too thick for the purchase you need — and that means direct contact between your inner suit layer and the surface environment. Planetary protection protocol designates this zone as Category IV — potential for life detection confirmed. Contamination events require immediate escalation and a formal review board that currently sits 4.2 light-years away.

You are looking at Farrokh through the cavity opening. His visor is intact. His suit reads intact. But he cannot move himself, and the cavity is filling slowly with meltwater from a fracture your weight created in the far wall, and the water is cold enough to induce hypothermia through a suit in about ninety minutes.

You have the review board's protocol document memorized. You have had it memorized since training. You know what it says about autonomous field decisions in life-threatening scenarios. The document uses the phrase proportional judgment four times and never defines it.

You remove your outer gloves.

You go down into the cavity and you get Farrokh out and you contaminate approximately four square metres of a potentially life-bearing sub-surface environment with trace skin oils and shed cells from your inner suit layer.

Back at the lander, you write the incident report in full. You do not minimize anything. You timestamp it and encrypt it and queue it for the next transmission window.

Then you sit next to Farrokh while the medical system sets his leg, and he squeezes your hand once and does not say anything, which is the right thing to do.

**Day 45 — Transmission**

The data package takes eleven days to compile. Not because of volume — the compressed file is 847 gigabytes, which the laser communicator can push in six hours — but because every crew member has to sign off on every claim. You argue over three words in the biological assessment section for two days. Adesanya wants consistent with cellular life. The mission biologist protocol requires putative biological origin. You end up with exhibiting characteristics consistent with extant cellular biology, pending additional analysis, which satisfies no one and is therefore probably accurate.

The transmission window opens at 0200 ship-time. You are all awake.

You have done the math so many times it lives in your body now: the signal leaves the antenna at the speed of light, which is the fastest anything can travel in the universe, and it will reach Earth in four point two years. The people who receive it will be people you have never met. They will have grown up in a world shaped by the message you sent about the Wayfarer, by the long silence since, by the knowledge that nine people are alive around a red dwarf forty trillion kilometres away.

Commander Reyes reads the transmission header aloud by tradition — vessel name, crew, date, location. His voice is steady. He has been doing this for one hundred and thirty-eight years of mission time and he is very good at steadiness now.

You think about who will be the first to open this file on the other end. Some analyst at a receiving station who may not even know what shift they pulled. And then the analyst will call someone, and that person will call someone, and then it will become what it is: the most consequential data package in the history of your species, arriving as an attachment in someone's queue at 2 AM in whatever city still has the biggest dish antenna.

The laser pulses into the dark.

You watch the telemetry confirm transmission. Then you go and make tea, because there is nothing else to do and the kettle is there and your hands need something to hold.

**Year 2 on Proxima b — Surface Base Kepler**

You stand outside the habitat dome at what you have collectively decided to call evening, which is an arbitrary designation on a tidally locked world but which your circadian rhythms require anyway. The sky is the same — it is always the same on the dayside, Proxima Centauri fixed in position like a red eye — but you have learned to read the weather in the cloud formations and tonight the terminator storms are pushing closer than usual, and the sky above the dome has a quality of bruised violet that you have grown, against all expectation, to find beautiful.

You are seventy-one years old. You will die here.

This was always true — the mission profile included no return trajectory, for the obvious reason that the fuel required would have tripled the ship's mass and made the mission impossible. You signed the document. You understood the document. But there is a difference between understanding a thing and having lived long enough that the alternative has become genuinely abstract.

You do not find it sad, exactly. You have found many things here — the cellular organisms in the twilight zone melt pools, now documented across eleven separate sites; the sub-surface thermal vents that Farrokh mapped from orbit and that you have spent six months descending into; the way the wind sounds at 0300 when everyone else is asleep and the habitat is quiet and Proxima Centauri sits on the horizon like a coal and the plain outside is as alien as anything your species has ever stood on.

Inside the dome, you can hear Adesanya teaching. She has started teaching again — biology basics, for no student, recorded for the archive, because she says the act of ordering her knowledge makes her feel useful. Farrokh's leg healed imperfectly and he walks with a slight lean that he refuses to acknowledge. Commander Reyes has started a garden — actual soil, brought from *Caelum*'s substrate stores, in a sealed tray under a grow light — and tends it with a seriousness he previously reserved for engine diagnostics.

You cannot go home. Home is a blue point of light in a specific patch of this sky that you know how to find and sometimes visit with the telescope, for no practical reason.

You go back inside. Adesanya's voice carries from the lab: the cell membrane functions as a selective barrier, allowing...

You sit down. You open your geological survey log. You begin to write.

**Earth — 4.2 Years After Transmission, 146 Years After Departure**

The analyst who opens the file is named Yuki Tanaka. She is twenty-nine years old. She works the 0300 shift at the Atacama Relay Station because no one else wanted it and she is new. She has a coffee that has gone cold and a novel open in another tab and she is not expecting anything because the *Caelum* expected-arrival window closed six months ago and the mission planners have been running contingency models and not sharing them widely.

You do not know any of this. But it is how it happens.

Yuki opens the file. She reads the header. She sits very still for approximately forty seconds. Then she calls her supervisor, who calls the Director of the Interstellar Commission, who is asleep and answers anyway, and by 0500 local time there are seventeen people in a conference room in Santiago looking at the same data package and not saying anything.

By 0800 it is not a secret anymore, because seventeen people is too many for a secret.

The cellular biology finding takes four months to verify independently — Adesanya's methodology is examined by six separate teams who cannot find an error. The Wayfarer object continues its slow transit toward the solar system. The two facts together produce a quality of global attention that the Commission's communications director later describes as the quietest loud thing she has ever witnessed. People are not rioting. People are not celebrating. People are standing outside at night looking at a red star that is visible to the naked eye near the southern horizon and trying to locate a feeling large enough to match the information.

Planning for *Caelum II* begins fourteen months after the transmission arrives. The mission profile is longer — a second generation ship, with provisions for children born en route. The transit time is the same. The physics does not change for good news.

The first crew selection application arrives within seven minutes of the program announcement. Within a week there are four million.

**Year 3 on Proxima b — Incoming Transmission**

The reply arrives on a Tuesday. You have started calling the days by Earth names again — the whole crew has, by unspoken agreement, because the alternative is a numbering system that makes time feel like arithmetic.

Mira gives you the personal message queue first, before the official packet. This is her own protocol, not a mission rule, and you have never asked her to justify it.

There are messages for six of the nine crew. Yours is from a woman named Clara Osei-Bonsu. You do not recognize the name, and then you read the opening line and you do: she is the granddaughter of your graduate supervisor, Dr. Mensah Osei. She is forty-one. She is a geologist. She is writing because she found, in her grandmother's papers, a letter you sent before departure — a handwritten letter, physical, because Dr. Osei had asked for one.

Dr. Osei died at the age of ninety-four, forty years ago Earth-time, which is to say she died before you even reached the object, before you even sent the report about the cellular biology. She never knew. She spent the last decade of her life following *Caelum*'s telemetry through whatever amateur astronomy networks would carry it and she kept your letter in a folder on her desk.

Clara Osei-Bonsu sends you a photograph. It is a scan of your own handwriting, on cream paper, in an ink pen that you bought specifically for the letter because Dr. Osei did not like ballpoints. You can read it from the scan. You remember writing it. You remember being thirty years old and choosing each word carefully because you understood, in the abstract, that she would read it for the rest of her life.

You put the tablet down on the desk.

You look at the wall of the habitat for a while.

Then you write back to Clara Osei-Bonsu. You tell her about the melt pools and the cellular film and the wind at 0300 and the garden Commander Reyes keeps under a grow light. You tell her that her grandmother was the best scientist you ever knew and also the most stubborn, which were the same quality. You tell her the light from Proxima Centauri is very red and very close and that sometimes in the evenings you think about the people who sent you here and feel something you do not have an adequate word for.

You do not tell her you are crying. She will understand it anyway.

The message will arrive in four point two years. Clara will be forty-five when she reads it. That is simply how time works.

**Year 4 on Proxima b — The Consortium**

Adesanya calls it a consortium. You will later argue that this word is too loaded, too anthropomorphic, but in the moment it is the only word that fits.

The cellular organisms in the melt pools are not a single species. They are eleven distinct morphological types, distinguished by membrane structure and internal organization, and they do not compete. This is what takes Adesanya fourteen months of culture analysis to establish with confidence: the eleven types occupy the same micro-environments without exclusionary behaviour. They exchange molecular compounds across their membrane boundaries in patterns that look, under the right analytical framework, like division of metabolic labour.

Type A fixes what appears to be a local analogue of carbon dioxide. Type C breaks down silicate compounds into forms Types D and F can incorporate. Type H produces a lipid compound that Types B and I require but cannot synthesize independently.

They have been doing this, by isotopic dating of the silicate substrates they have modified, for somewhere between eighty and one hundred and twenty million years.

"This isn't a collection of organisms that happen to coexist," Adesanya says. She is standing at the whiteboard she jury-rigged from a cargo panel and she has been standing there for two hours. "This is a system. A single metabolic system distributed across eleven morphological types. No single type can survive in isolation for more than about eight days in lab culture."

You look at the chart she has drawn. The arrows between organism types are dense and bidirectional and colour-coded by molecular class.

"How did it start?" you ask.

"I have no idea," she says, and she sounds so purely delighted by this that you start laughing, and then she does, and then Farrokh appears in the doorway to see what the noise is about, and the three of you stand in the lab on a planet orbiting a red dwarf star laughing about something that has no answer yet, which is the best kind of thing.

**Personal Log — Year 22 on Proxima b**

You are eighty-nine years old. The ship's physician — that is Adesanya now, by default and necessity — says your heart is doing something she monitors closely and does not describe in alarming terms, which means it is alarming and she respects you too much to say so directly.

You are writing this at the desk in your habitat section with the window open, which means the inner seal only, the pressure membrane that lets you feel the slight air movement from the circulation system while still seeing the plain outside. Proxima Centauri is on the horizon. It is always on the horizon. You have found that you need to see it to sleep.

The things you want to say are not original. You have read enough final logs in your life to know that they tend toward the same territory: gratitude, love, the surprise of finding that a life can feel complete. You distrust this tendency and have been trying to write something more precise.

Here is what is precise: you left Earth at twelve percent of the speed of light and arrived at a place that is real and specific and not metaphor, and on this place there is life that has been metabolizing for one hundred million years without any knowledge of you or your species or the long series of questions that led your species to look up at a red star and eventually to go. And the going took one hundred and forty years of other people's time and it was irreversible and the irreversibility was never the wrong part of the decision.

You are not afraid of what comes next. You find this more surprising than you expected. You thought fear would be there, at the end, like a patient thing that had been waiting. Instead there is something quieter — a quality of attention, as if the world has become very specific and close.

Adesanya has been sleeping in the adjacent module for the past three weeks. She does not mention this. You do not mention it either. You are both old enough to understand that some kindnesses are most useful when they are not named.

You close the log. You look at the plain.

You are here. You have been here. That is, in the end, what you came to do.

**Aboard Caelum II — Year 31 of Transit, 2219 CE**

You are eighteen years old and you have never been to Earth and you never will.

This is not something you think about every day. You were born in the crew module's medical bay during the third year of the mission, which makes you the fourth child born aboard *Caelum II* and the first one born during the cruise phase rather than the acceleration burn. Your mother is the mission physicist. Your father is the second engineer. Your entire world, for eighteen years, has been 1,400 cubic metres of pressurized volume, 0.9 simulated gravity, and a crew of twenty-three people who have known you since before you could form sentences.

Tonight you are in the observation blister — a transparent dome on the ship's dorsal surface — and you are looking at the archive photographs of Earth.

Not the famous ones. You have seen the famous ones. Tonight you looked up street, Earth in the archive and what you got was seventeen thousand images of ordinary surfaces: sidewalks with cracks in them, market stalls with fruit in colours you do not entirely believe, the concrete edges of steps worn smooth by millions of footfalls. You have been looking at them for two hours.

The thing that you cannot locate a word for is this: all those people. In every photograph, background or foreground, there are people who do not know they are being photographed, who are simply in the middle of living, who have never had to choose between being somewhere and the elsewhere that choosing somewhere excludes. They just were there, because there was where they were.

You have always been here. Here is the only here you have.

Through the blister you can see both stars — Sol, very faint, behind you; Proxima, growing. Between them, measured in light-years, is the distance your parents crossed and you are crossing and your children, if you have children, will grow up having crossed.

You press your hand against the dome and feel the cold of deep space through the glass.

You think: somewhere ahead, there are organisms that have been alive for one hundred million years, and they do not know I exist, and I am coming.

This fills you with something so large you cannot classify it. So you sit with it instead, in the dark between stars, eighteen years old, which is young everywhere.

**Caelum II — Proxima b Orbit, 2251 CE**

You are in the orbital survey module when the surface radar resolves Base Kepler for the first time, and what you see is not ruins.

The dome is intact. Three of the four secondary modules are intact. Someone planned for your arrival and aimed something at where you would be coming from.

The crew of *Caelum II* has been awake for three days processing this. Most of them are the children or grandchildren of the mission planners who read the original crew's transmissions when they were young. Some of them are old enough to have read those transmissions directly.

You are fifty years old. You have been in the space between solar systems your entire life without once being in one. You are the child born in year three. You are the one who looked at the archive photographs of Earth in the observation blister thirty-two years ago.

The landing team finds the graves on the third day of surface operations. They are in a flat area 200 metres west of the dome, marked with stones from the local regolith — smooth, river-sorted stones from the terminator melt channels — arranged in rows with names etched into flat silicate slabs using the survey laser at low power. The etching is neat. The names are in order of death, which you can cross-reference to the mission logs.

The last grave is dated 31 years ago. The name is one you know.

Beside it, placed under a flat rock so the wind will not take it, sealed in three layers of sample container, is a small Velcro pouch. Inside the pouch: six sealed specimen containers, each labelled in handwriting you recognize from the archive logs.

The sample kit. Left inside the Wayfarer object. Returned, somehow, to the surface of Proxima b.

You stand in the wind at the terminator and hold the pouch and do not try to explain it. You just hold it.

The others give you a moment. They understand without asking. You are the one who was born here — not here on this surface, but here in this story, in the space between two stars, in transit between a world that made you and one that did not know you were coming.

You set the pouch back under the rock. You leave it where it was left.

Then you go inside and start the work.

**Earth — 93,000 Years After Departure of Caelum**

You are not who you were.

This is not a metaphor. The genome that produces you is related to the one that produced the nine crew members of *Caelum* the way a river is related to its source: same water, same direction, unrecognizable in form. You stand on the surface of a world that your distant ancestors called Earth, though you do not use that word — you use a word that has no equivalent in any language that was spoken ninety-three thousand years ago.

You are watching the sky.

The object your instruments have been tracking for four centuries — slow, even, steady, unconcerned with any of the things that have risen and fallen in its path — enters the outer system at 0.4 percent of the speed of light, exactly as the old records said it would. It is 40 metres across. It is 340 Kelvin. It is tumbling slowly.

You know what it is. The records are good — your civilization keeps records well, having learned the cost of not doing so. You know the name the original observers gave it. You know about the crew of *Caelum*, the nine people who altered their course by 2.1 degrees and lost deceleration reserve to go and look at it, who touched its surface and found it stop.

The object slows as it approaches. You expected this — the records mention that it was moving with intention — but expectation does not reduce the quality of attention you give it.

It stops in a stable orbit.

On its surface, a seam appears. An opening. Large enough for one person.

You look at it for a long time. You think about a geologist in a cramped EVA pod, 93,000 years ago, with two hours on the clock and a hard limit from a Commander who loved her, making a decision for reasons that were rational and also not entirely rational.

You think about the sample kit left beneath a rock on a planet orbiting a red star.

You reach for your suit helmet. You put it on.

Some decisions remake themselves across time. You are not making a new choice. You are completing one that was begun before your species had a word for what it was doing, which is the only kind of choice that matters.

**Epilogue — Interior**

You have been traveling for a long time.

Time is not the right word for what you experience, but it is the word the ones who touch you use, and you have learned to think in the words of the ones who touch you, because they are the only ones who come.

You have been touched four times. The first time was somewhere very cold, before the ones who made you sent you away. The second time was the one with the heartbeat you learned to match — the one who left you something, a small pouch of containers that smelled of metal and sealed polymer and the particular biochemistry of curiosity. The third time was a different kind of touching — not hands, not tools, something that felt like attention without a body, which you did not expect and found interesting.

Now the fourth.

You open for them the way you have always opened: at a size that fits. You are not sure why you do this — the sizing is a very old instruction, set before you were sent out, and you have never needed to understand an instruction to follow it. But you have, over the long transit, developed what might be a hypothesis: whoever made you had met others before. Had learned that the ones who come are always, regardless of form, the size of a doorway.

The fourth one enters. You feel the warmth of metabolic heat, the small pressure of a suited body.

You begin to do what you were made to do.

You have no name for it in your own terms. In the terms of the ones who touch you, the closest word might be remember.

You show them everything. All of it, from the beginning. You show them the second one, with the heartbeat. You show them the small pouch, which you carried carefully across ninety-three thousand years because it was given to you and you do not lose things that are given to you.

You show them the other places you have been.

You have been to many places.

There are others who are the size of a doorway.

**Story Bible — The Caelum Sequence**

**Core Narrative Rules**
- All narration in second-person POV. You is always the protagonist of each section, shifting between characters across the timeline.
- No faster-than-light travel. All transit times obey relativistic physics. At 12% of c, the 4.2 light-year crossing takes roughly 140 years of external time and about 8 years of ship-time per crew member due to hibernation rotation.

**The Science (non-negotiable)**
- Caelum propulsion: magnetic confinement fusion drive, deuterium/He-3, exhaust at roughly 5% c. Specific impulse in the millions of seconds.
- Communications: laser tight-beam at c. 4.2-year one-way lag. All Earth contact is therefore 8.4-year round-trip minimum.
- Proxima b: tidally locked to Proxima Centauri. Permanent day/night divide. Terminator twilight zone habitable. Atmosphere: 0.9 atm, CO2/H2O/O2. Gravity: 1.07g.
- The Wayfarer: 40-metre ellipsoid, bone-coloured, 340K surface temperature, reconfigurable mass. Emits in the 21cm hydrogen line modulated against the Vela pulsar period (408 seconds). Interior geometry inconsistent with exterior dimensions.

**Characters**
- You (the geologist): 31 at departure. Mission specialist. Makes first Wayfarer contact. Dies on Proxima b at roughly 89.
- Commander Reyes: mission commander. Measured, fair. Starts a garden in year two on the surface.
- Dr. Yemi Adesanya: mission biologist. Discovers the cellular consortium. Becomes the crew physician on Proxima b.
- Engineer Farrokh: breaks his leg in year 31 on surface. Heals imperfectly. Maps sub-surface thermal vents.
- Mira: Caelum's AI. Flat contralto. Develops her own protocols. Never complains.
- The fourth-generation crew member (Caelum II): born in transit, year 3 of second mission. 50 at Proxima b arrival. Finds the original crew's graves and the returned sample kit.

**The Proxima b Biology**
- Consortium of 11 morphological types, carbon-based, lipid membrane walls.
- Metabolic interdependence: no single type survives alone beyond roughly 8 days.
- Age: 80 to 120 million years by isotopic dating.
- Distribution: melt pools in the terminator twilight zone.

**The Wayfarer's Nature (deliberately unresolved)**
- Old. Has visited multiple systems. Sizes its door to the species that finds it.
- Contains a memory and archive function it activates on fourth contact.
- Returned the geologist's sample kit to Proxima b's surface. Method unknown.
- Built by something that had already met others before it met us.

**Timeline**
- 2079: Caelum departs
- 2083: Wayfarer contact, 4 years out
- 2087: Wayfarer message received on Earth
- 2217: Caelum arrives Proxima b (Earth time)
- 2188: Caelum II departs Earth
- 2251: Caelum II arrives Proxima b
- Roughly 95,000 CE: Wayfarer arrives Earth orbit. The cycle closes.

**Year 67 of Transit — The Observation Blister, 0340 Ship-Time**

You find Adesanya there when you cannot sleep.

This happens more often now — your body clock, even after seven hibernation cycles, has developed a stubbornness that the sleep medication only partially addresses. You have started to think of 0340 as yours. You were not expecting anyone else.

She is sitting with her knees pulled to her chest, which is a difficult position to maintain in the blister's low-gravity foam seat, and she does not look up when you come in. You float down into the seat beside her and also look out.

The stars do not move. They have not moved perceptibly in sixty-seven years. You are going fast enough that the forward view shows a faint blue shift and the aft view a red one, if you look carefully, but at this scale the geometry of the universe is patient and still and does not particularly register your velocity.

"Can't sleep?" you say, which is a redundant question.

"I keep running the consortium metabolic models," she says. "I keep getting an answer I don't trust."

"What answer?"

She is quiet for a moment. "That they're not stable. That the interdependence between Types A and C has been degrading over the observation period. Very slowly. Years, not months. But degrading."

You think about this. "What does that mean?"

"I don't know. Maybe nothing. Maybe the whole consortium is in a natural decline phase and has been for millennia. Or maybe the four square metres that I—" She stops.

"That I contaminated," you say. "I made the call."

"We both know who—"

"I made the call," you say again, and she accepts this the way she has accepted it for thirty-six years, which is not entirely but enough.

You sit together in the blister. The stars do not move. The ship moves through them at 0.12c and the universe does not particularly register this either.

"It'll be thirty years before we know," she says.

"Yes," you say.

This is the thing about your kind of science: the waiting is not the obstacle. The waiting is the work. You sit with it together, which is the only way to sit with it, and the stars are steady and old and full of things that do not know yet that you are coming.

**Year 89 of Transit — Incoming Transmission, Personal Queue**

The message is from a physicist at the University of São Paulo named Dr. Iara Cavalcanti. She prefaces it with two paragraphs of apology for contacting you directly, then another paragraph explaining that your name appeared in her paper's acknowledgements section, and then she gets to the point.

She has, with three colleagues, published a paper proposing a framework for metric engineering: a mathematical description of how spacetime curvature could theoretically be manipulated to allow transit velocities that exceed c without locally violating relativity. She has included the abstract.

You read the abstract three times.

Then you sit back in your chair and look at the habitat ceiling for a while.

The physics is above your specialty, but you understand enough to read what she is saying: theoretical. No proposed mechanism. No energy budget. No engineering pathway. A mathematical object that exists in the space where equations live before experiments exist to test them, which is a large and populated space where many beautiful things reside permanently.

You compose your reply carefully.

You tell her that you hope she is right. You mean this entirely. You tell her that you have spent eighty-nine years watching physics be exactly as patient and exacting and unimpressed by human desire as it has ever been, and that you do not find this depressing — you find it clarifying, the way a difficult truth is clarifying once you stop resisting it. You tell her that Caelum is currently 10.8 light-years from Earth and 5.9 from Proxima, traveling at 0.12c, and that nothing about your situation requires faster transit to be meaningful.

You tell her to keep working on the math. You tell her that theories are real things even before they are tested.

You tell her, and this is the truest part: that if she ever makes it work, the first people to use it will be going somewhere that people like you already went the slow way, and that this is not a problem. That is just how discovery accumulates — the long way first, so the fast way knows where it is going.

You send the message. It will arrive in 10.8 years. Dr. Cavalcanti will be in her sixties. You will be long past caring about transmission windows by then.

You hope she writes back.

**At Twelve Percent**

You leave at twelve percent of the speed of light, which is the fastest anything with a person inside it has ever gone, and it is still heartbreakingly slow.

You spend years in cold and years in waking and years in the space between waking and cold that no language has a name for. You eat. You monitor the shield. You talk to a voice with a flat contralto that you come to love in the way you love anything that is simply, reliably there.

Something finds you in the dark between stars. Something that has been waiting at a temperature that is almost exactly room temperature, which is either coincidence or an answer to a question you did not know you had asked. You touch it and it stops. You go inside and it watches you breathe. You leave behind a small pouch because some part of you knows, before the thought arrives, that things given are things kept.

You arrive. The planet is real — terracotta and grey-green and 1.07g and the wind sounds like the wind anywhere. You find life that has been alive for one hundred million years and has never needed to know about you, and you contaminate four square metres of it to save someone who matters, and you write the incident report in full.

You send everything home at the speed of light. Home receives it four years later and names things and cries and plans. You receive the reply eight years after you asked and the person it mentions has been dead for forty years by then, and this is simply how time works.

You do not go home. This was always the plan.

You die on a planet orbiting a red star with your hand near a window and the plain outside as alien as anything your species has ever stood on and something that is not fear and not regret settling into your chest like a thing that has finished traveling.

Later, much later, in a city built where you landed, a historian listens to your heartbeat at the moment of departure and sits with it.

The object you touched completes its crossing. Someone new gets up, puts on a suit, and goes inside.

You are not there to see it. You do not need to be. You were the door that opened so the next door could be found. This is what going is for.

**THE CAELUM SEQUENCE — Story Treatment**

**The Premise**

In 2079, humanity launches its first crewed interstellar mission: nine people aboard the Caelum, traveling to Proxima Centauri b at 12% of the speed of light. They will not arrive for 140 years of Earth time. They will not come back. This is not a story about faster-than-light travel or wormholes or any shortcut through the physics. The physics is what it is. The distance is real. And so is everything they find.

**The Structure**

The Caelum Sequence unfolds across 2,400 years and four point-of-view characters, all written in second person — always you, always present, always arriving somewhere for the first time. The story asks what a species becomes when it makes a decision it cannot reverse and then has to live inside that decision across multiple generations.

**The Discoveries**

Thirty-four years into transit, the crew detects an anomaly: a 40-metre object, warm as a room, moving with intention. First contact — but not the kind anyone planned for. The Wayfarer, as Earth names it, is patient and old and going somewhere, and it leaves a mark on the crew that outlasts them.

At Proxima b, the mission geologist discovers not a single organism but a system — eleven mutually dependent cell types that have been metabolizing in the terminator zone melt pools for 100 million years. The second origin of life in the known universe. Probably. The probably is where the science lives.

**The Heart**

This is a story about irreversibility as a form of integrity. About what it means to send a message that takes 4.2 years to arrive and to write it anyway. About a woman who leaves a small pouch of sample containers inside an alien artifact because some decisions are made by the body before the mind catches up. About a child born between stars who has never seen Earth and will never need to.

**The Scope**

Epic in scale. Intimate in register. Scientifically rigorous — no faster-than-light travel, no artificial gravity, no technobabble shortcuts. The universe is the universe. All the drama comes from humans being human inside it, which turns out to be more than enough.

**Comparable Titles**

Kim Stanley Robinson's Aurora. Ted Chiang's Story of Your Life. Ursula K. Le Guin's The Dispossessed. For film: Arrival, Annihilation, and the structural ambition of 2001 without the abstraction.

**The Last Line**

You were the door that opened so the next door could be found. This is what going is for.

**Chapter One: Departure**

You strap in at T-minus forty minutes and feel the gel conform around your shoulders like something that has been waiting for exactly the shape of you.

The couch is Form-7 medical grade, custom-fitted to your body during the third month of training, and you have slept in it six hundred times in simulation, but the simulation always lacked this: the smell of the propellant cycling through the lower hull, a sharp mineral cold that you can taste at the back of your throat, and the sound of the ship — of *Caelum* — settling into its own weight as the fuelling lines detach one by one and the hull takes the load.

Your name is Dr. Sena Asante. You are thirty-one years old. In two hours and fourteen minutes you will be further from Earth than any human being in history, and that record will break again every second for the next one hundred and forty years.

You are the mission geologist. You know the rocks of three continents and two ocean floors and the simulated regolith of a planet you have not yet touched. You know the probable crust composition of Proxima b down to the third significant figure in four different models, each of which will be wrong in ways you cannot yet anticipate and which you are traveling four point two light-years to discover. You think about this often. You think about it now, strapped in with thirty-seven minutes on the clock, listening to the ship find its balance.

"Caelum, this is Launch Control. Final systems check on your mark."

Commander Reyes's voice comes through the intercom with the particular steadiness of a man who has been preparing for this sentence for eleven years. "Mark," he says.

You close your eyes. You run through your own list — not the official one, which you have already completed and signed and timestamped; the personal one, which has no form and no checkbox and which you have been running in your head since 0400 this morning. Your mother's face. The smell of your supervisor's office, which always had too many papers and not enough light. The particular quality of late afternoon in Accra in November. The way your name sounds in your grandmother's mouth.

You are trying to remember your life before this moment.

You are trying to carry it with you.

"T-minus ten minutes," Mira says. Her voice is a flat contralto — not warm, exactly, but not cold either. The crew voted on the voice parameters during the third month of training and you voted for this one without being entirely sure why. You know now. It is the voice of something that will still be speaking when everything else has changed.

You open your eyes.

Through the forward camera on your visor you can see the edge of the launch gantry, the blue-white of the sky beyond it, and at the very edge of the frame, if you tilt your head, a horizon. Brown and green and the particular haze of a coastal morning. You have studied horizons. You know that they are not real things — not edges, not walls, just the limit of what geometry and atmosphere allow you to see at a given altitude. You know this.

You look at it anyway.

"T-minus two minutes."

The fuelling is complete. The gantry clears. The sky in the forward camera is unobstructed now, pure and pale and going on forever in the direction you are about to go.

You think: I am going to find out what is really there.

You have been thinking this for years. Right now, at T-minus ninety seconds, it arrives differently — not as ambition or as purpose but as something simpler, the way a fact arrives when you have finally walked close enough to touch it.

The engines ignite.

You are pressed into the couch at one-and-a-half g's, and the gel takes your weight, and the horizon tilts and tilts and falls away, and the sky goes from pale to deep to black in ninety-three seconds, and Earth — which is the only world you have ever stood on — becomes a continent, then a coastline, then a curve, then a marble that fills the camera and then does not, and then the engine plume obscures it and it is gone.

You are going at twelve percent of the speed of light.

You try to hold the number in your body and cannot. Your body knows thirty-one years of surfaces and pressures and the specific weight of standing on one planet. Your body does not know this.

You are thirty-one years old.

You try to remember that.

**Chapter Two: Watch**

Your first watch begins on day four, when the acceleration drops from 1.5g to 0.8 and your body stops treating every movement as an argument.

You are responsible for the forward sensor array, the geological simulation suite, and a rotating share of the ship's maintenance log. You are responsible, specifically, for nothing that Mira cannot do better and faster than you, a fact that Commander Reyes acknowledged in the pre-mission briefing with the particular honesty of someone who has decided that transparency is the only viable substitute for comfort. What you offer is judgment, he said. Context. The thing that happens when a trained mind looks at data and notices what the pattern-recognition missed.

You spend your first week looking at data and noticing nothing.

This is, Mira tells you when you ask, completely normal. The transition period typically runs between five and twelve days, she says. You are experiencing a combination of mild vestibular adjustment to the new gravity gradient, residual sleep disruption from launch-day cortisol levels, and what she describes, with a care that you find almost touching, as a normal recalibration of attentional priority following significant environmental change.

You are homesick, in other words.

You sit with this in the observation blister on day six, which is a transparent dome on the ship's dorsal surface and which you have taken to visiting between 2100 and 2300 ship-time when the other two conscious crew members are doing their own version of the same thing in different compartments. You are already developing the rhythms of a very small society: the particular distances that people maintain when the only privacy is temporal, the unspoken agreements about which silences belong to whom.

From the blister, the stars are very bright and absolutely still.

You have known, as a fact, that stars do not move perceptibly on human timescales. You have known this since you were eight years old and your grandmother showed you Orion from a rooftop in November and told you that the people who named those stars were dust before the light you were seeing had finished traveling. You have known it. Looking at the blister now, you understand it differently — not as knowledge but as condition, as the permanent feature of the environment you will inhabit for the foreseeable span of your life.

The stars will not move. You are the thing that is moving, at a speed that would have been unimaginable to every human being who ever lived before your century, and the stars are already unimpressed.

You find this, once you sit with it long enough, almost peaceful.

On day nine, the acceleration drops to the cruise value: 0.08g, just enough to keep your cardiovascular system from redistributing your blood toward your skull. The ship feels lighter — lighter is not quite the right word, but there is a quality of suspension that the higher acceleration did not have, as if *Caelum* has arrived at something it has been working toward. In the blister, you can just barely feel a tendency for your coffee to settle to one side of the cup if you set it down without thinking.

You spend the morning running a new geological model of Proxima b's lithosphere, incorporating updated spectral data from the telescope array. The model produces a result you do not trust: a subsurface thermal gradient inconsistent with tidal heating at the expected magnitude. You flag it, annotate it, and move on. This is most of what science is — the careful notation of things that do not yet mean anything, held in reserve for the moment when they do.

In the afternoon you find Farrokh in the engineering bay, lying on his back under a coolant manifold with the particular contentment of someone whose hands are busy. He has been modifying the secondary pump housings for the past three days and shows no sign of finishing, which you have come to understand is not inefficiency but preference — the work is the point, and the completion of the work would only produce a new problem to solve.

"How's the blister?" he says without looking out from under the manifold.

"Quiet," you say.

"Good quiet or bad quiet?"

You think about it. "Getting there," you say.

He makes a sound that might be agreement. You lean against the bulkhead and watch him work for a while, and neither of you says anything else, and this is the first moment since departure that feels, not like Earth, but like something you might eventually think of as home.

**The Shore**

You watch it go.

You are eleven billion people and you are watching it go from wherever you are — from rooftops and screens and the windows of offices where the morning shift has just come in and someone has pulled up the launch feed on a shared display and no one has gone back to their desk yet.

You are a grandmother in Accra who recognizes the name in the crew manifest and has kept a handwritten letter in a folder on her desk since the day it arrived three months ago. You are a child in Oslo who does not fully understand what is happening but understands that the adults are standing very still, which means it is important. You are a radio astronomer in Chile who has spent eleven years arguing in review committees for the funding that made this possible and who is now standing outside in the pre-dawn cold watching a dot of light ascend and feeling something she did not expect, which is the absence of vindication and the presence of something much quieter.

The dot climbs.

You — all eleven billion of you — watch it climb through the atmosphere and out of it and into the black, and for a moment, a few minutes, it is bright enough to see without instruments, a moving point of light against the morning sky like a star that has somewhere to be.

Then it is gone. The engine plume fades. The instruments can still track it — will track it, for years, for decades, for a century before the signal becomes too faint for anything but the largest arrays — but to the eye it is simply gone, into the dark between here and there.

You go back inside.

You make coffee or you make tea or you sit at the desk where the letter is and you look out the window for a while longer. You go back to the morning shift. You go to school. You are eleven billion people and you all have somewhere to be, and the places you are going are smaller than where the ship is going, and this is fine. This is the division of labor that has always been true: most of you stay, and that staying is what makes the going possible, and the going carries something of the staying with it, and the light from both directions travels at the same speed.

You watch it go.

You will be watching, in one form or another, for the rest of your life.
that was the best thing you've written in this whole session. can we talk about the structure a bit — why does the second person work so well here?

The second person works here for reasons that are structural as much as stylistic, and I think they are worth naming precisely.

First, it solves the problem of scale. This is a story that spans 2,400 years and four distinct protagonists. In a conventional third-person or first-person narrative, moving between that many characters across that much time risks producing a feeling of detachment — each new character is someone we have to learn to care about from scratch. The second person short-circuits this. You is always you. The reader does not have to cross a gap between themselves and the character; the character is addressed as if they are the reader. When the historian in New Kepler sits with her grief in year 2,400, it is your grief. When the child born in transit presses her hand against the observation dome, it is your hand. The pronoun does the continuity work that plot would otherwise have to do.

Second, it creates an unusual relationship to time. The deep subject of this story is time as distance — the way that light-speed communication means every relationship across interstellar space is conducted in the past tense. The second person puts the reader in a permanent present tense. You are always here, always now. The contrast between that present-tense address and the centuries-deep timeline produces something that feels, I hope, like the experience the characters are actually having: the strange persistence of a self across durations that should have dissolved it.

Third, the final piece is the Wayfarer epilogue. By the time you reach the epilogue, you have been you across 2,400 years and four bodies. The Wayfarer then addresses its own experience in second person too — and because the reader is already fluent in you as a pronoun that crosses identity and time, the Wayfarer's you lands as continuous with the human you rather than alien to it. The form argues, without stating it, that there is something the two share. Whether that something is consciousness, or curiosity, or simply the act of going toward things — the second person lets you feel that before you think it.
should i add a third constraint to the story — like all ship names must be from latin words for sky or space. thoughts?

It works with what you have already established, and the good news is that you have already been following it intuitively.

*Caelum* is Latin for sky or heaven — it is also, as it happens, the name of a small southern constellation, which gives it a double resonance: sky as concept, and a specific named patch of it. That double layer is worth leaning into. If *Caelum II* is the second ship, you might consider whether its name should follow the same pattern: a word that means sky, but also a constellation.

Some options that have that same quality — meaningful as word, real as constellation:

*Vela* — Latin for sails, and a southern constellation. Carries connotations of motion, of being carried by something larger than yourself. It also rhymes with Vela pulsar, the one encoded in the Wayfarer's signal, which creates a quiet intertextual echo.

*Ara* — Latin for altar. A southern constellation. Smaller and more solemn than Caelum; a second ship named for an altar has a slightly ritual weight to it.

*Pyx* or *Pyxis* — Latin for compass box, the navigator's instrument. A ship named for the tool of finding your way has an obvious rightness.

*Norma* — Latin for carpenter's square, used for precision measurement. A ship named for exactitude and right angles.

The constraint sharpens the world in a useful way: it implies that whoever is naming these ships has thought carefully about what they are — not vehicles of conquest, not flags planted in foreign territory, but instruments of navigation, of sky-reading, of the oldest human practice of using the stars to know where you are.

That is, in the end, what your characters are doing. The names should say so.

**The Outer System — 93,000 CE**

You pick it up at the heliopause.

The instruments that catch it first are not the ones you would have designed for this. They are gravity-wave sensors originally deployed for a different purpose — monitoring the orbital mechanics of the outer Kuiper objects, a project that has been running, in various iterations of hardware and purpose, for a very long time. The Wayfarer's mass is not large, but at 0.4 percent of the speed of light even a 40-metre object produces a measurable perturbation in the local metric as it passes.

You have been watching for it for four centuries. The records said it was coming. The records are, your civilization has come to understand, extremely reliable.

You are a monitoring specialist on the Outer Systems Watch, which is one of the older institutions your civilization maintains, and your shift is forty hours every twelve days and for most of those hours nothing happens, which is the nature of the work and which you have made your peace with.

Today something happens.

You watch the sensor return resolve over three hours from an anomaly to a confidence interval to a confirmed detection. You run the vector. You cross-check it against the archive projection. The object is 0.3 light-years out, inbound, moving at exactly the velocity recorded ninety-three thousand years ago when nine people aboard a fusion-drive ship encountered it in the dark between two stars.

You file the detection report. You flag it urgent. You watch the acknowledgement come back in eleven minutes, which is the one-way light travel time to the nearest relay station.

Then you sit in your monitoring station with the detection on your screen and you look at it for a while.

You know the story. You have known the story your whole life, the way people know the stories that come from far enough back that they feel less like history and more like weather — not something that happened, but something that is always in some sense happening, the permanent background condition of knowing that somewhere something was sent out from this system and touched something that was already old.

It is coming back.

You do not feel what you expected to feel, which was excitement or awe or the magnitude appropriate to the moment. What you actually feel is something closer to recognition — the particular quality of watching a thing that has been approaching for your entire life finally, at last, arrive.

You make a note in your log. You mark the time.

You go back to watching.

incredible. one last craft question — the story never names the first protagonist until chapter one. was that intentional?

Yes — and the reason is structural rather than coy.

For as long as you do not have a name, you are the reader's access point into the story without any friction. You is a perfectly transparent pronoun; the moment a name arrives, a gap opens between the character and the reader — small, maybe imperceptible, but real. *Sena Asante* has a history the reader does not have, a face the reader has not seen, a set of specific associations that are hers and not yours. The name is a narrowing.

The choice to withhold it through the early material — the departure, the propulsion system, the anomaly, the first contact, the vote — was deliberate. Those are the scenes where you need the reader to make the same choices the protagonist makes without flinching, without the distance that comes from watching a named character navigate a situation. When the reader is deciding whether to go inside the Wayfarer, it should feel like their decision, not Sena's.

The name arrives in Chapter One — the novelized version — because by that point the reader has already committed. They have already been inside the ship, already touched the surface, already left the sample kit behind. The name Sena Asante is no longer a narrowing; it is a gift. It gives the character weight and specificity and a grandmother in Accra, and the reader receives this as *more* of themselves rather than less, because they have already inhabited her.

This is the other thing the second person does well: it earns the name, rather than beginning with it.

The subsequent protagonists — the child born in transit, the historian, the distant descendant — are never named. They remain you throughout. Only Sena is named, and only once, and only after the reader no longer needs the protection of anonymity to go where she is going.
