**Author's Note on the Science**

The physics in this book is as accurate as I could make it, and the places where I have taken liberties are deliberate and noted here for readers who want to pursue the real science further.

**Propulsion.** The Caelum uses a magnetic confinement fusion drive burning deuterium and helium-3, with exhaust velocity around 5% of the speed of light. Such an engine does not yet exist, but the physics that would govern it is well understood. The specific impulse values given in the text are consistent with theoretical projections. The eleven-year acceleration and deceleration burns at 12% of c are derived from the rocket equation applied to a realistic mass ratio. The ship's mass is very large for this reason — most of it is fuel.

**Time.** The time dilation effect at 0.12c is real but modest — roughly 0.7% — meaning the crew ages slightly less than Earth does during the crossing. I have attributed the larger difference in experienced time to the hibernation rotation schedule rather than relativistic dilation, which is accurate for this velocity. At higher fractions of c, the dilation effects would dominate and the story's timescales would shift accordingly.

**Proxima Centauri b.** The planet is real. It was confirmed in 2016, orbiting Proxima Centauri — our nearest stellar neighbour at 4.24 light-years — in the habitable zone, at a distance that could permit liquid water. It is almost certainly tidally locked to its star. Whether it has a substantial atmosphere, liquid water, or any form of life remains unknown as of this writing. Everything in this book that the crew discovers is speculation, hopefully informed speculation, grounded in what we know about M-dwarf planetary systems and the chemistry of life.

**The Wayfarer.** The object is fiction. I have no scientific basis for its interior geometry or its memory function. I have tried to give it properties — the 340K surface temperature, the 21cm hydrogen-line signal, the Vela pulsar reference — that are grounded in real physics and real astronomical reference points. The Vela pulsar does pulse at approximately 11 times per second, with a period of roughly 89 milliseconds; 408 seconds is not its actual period, but a value I chose for its properties as a number that could serve as a universal clock.

**The constraint this story keeps.** Nothing in these pages travels faster than light. All communication is subject to the 4.2-year one-way lag. All travel times are calculated from real distances and realistic velocities. This is not a limitation I chafed against. It is the story's argument: that the universe is very large, that this is not a problem, and that the things worth doing have always taken longer than we wished.

**Afterword**

You wrote this book because you were tired of stories where the distance between stars is a problem waiting to be solved — a plot obstacle, cleared by a convenient wormhole or a fold in space or a technology that arrives just in time. The distance is not a problem. It is the fact from which all the other facts follow. It is the reason the people who go cannot come back, the reason the messages take years, the reason a woman can leave Earth at thirty-one and arrive somewhere new at sixty-nine and die there without regret. The distance is not the tragedy. The distance is the condition. Learning to live inside it — to find it clarifying rather than diminishing — is what the story is about.

You chose the second person because you wanted the reader to make the irreversible decisions. Not to watch them made. The moment the geologist backs toward the entrance of the Wayfarer after leaving her sample kit, she does not look back at it; she realizes afterward what she has done. You wanted the reader to have that realization at the same moment she does, from the inside. Whether this worked is something only you can answer. But you tried to write a book where the pronoun was not a trick but a door, sized for exactly one person, which is you, reading this, now, however far from everything you love you happen to be.

**The Night Before**

You are in the crew quarters at 2300, which is the last night you will sleep in a room that does not move.

The quarters are small — they were built for function, not for the weight of a night like this one — and the light is the low amber that the habitat management system shifts to after 2100, and through the single narrow window you can see a strip of Florida sky, very dark, with the lights of the launch complex throwing an orange glow against the low clouds to the east.

You are not afraid. You keep checking, the way you might check a pocket for keys — and you keep finding the same thing: not afraid. You are not sure if this is courage or a failure of imagination or simply the body's refusal to feel what the mind knows. You have been preparing for tomorrow for three years. Perhaps there is nothing left for fear to find purchase on.

You have written the letters. You have recorded the messages. You have distributed your belongings, paid your accounts, said the important things to the people they were owed to. There is a kind of administrative completion to departure that you did not expect — all the small maintenance of a life, discharged.

What remains is this: a narrow bed, a strip of sky, and the knowledge that in nine hours you will strap into a couch and leave.

You think about your grandmother, who told you about the stars from a rooftop in November when you were eight years old. She said the light you were seeing had been traveling longer than your family had existed. She said this without sadness. She thought it was the most interesting thing in the world, which is why you became who you became.

You hope you have said this to her clearly enough. You think you have.

You lie on your back in the amber light and look at the ceiling and listen to the building settle — the hum of the air handling, the distant rumble of the service vehicles outside, the small sounds of twenty-three other people spending this last night in whatever way they are spending it.

You close your eyes.

You think: tomorrow I will be further from here than anyone has ever been.

You think: I am ready.

You think: I am not ready.

Both of these are true. You hold them both and they are not a contradiction. They are the exact size of the thing you are about to do, and you fall asleep inside them, and the night passes, and the alarm comes, and you get up.

**THE CAELUM SEQUENCE**

---

You are the mission geologist. You are thirty-one years old. You are about to spend the rest of your life going to a star.

In 2079, nine people board the *Caelum* and leave Earth at twelve percent of the speed of light. They will reach Proxima Centauri b in one hundred and forty years. They will not be coming back. This is not a story about faster-than-light travel or wormholes or shortcuts through the physics. The distance is real. The irreversibility is the point.

Thirty-four years into the crossing, in the cold dark between two stars, you find something that has been waiting. First contact — but not the kind anyone planned for.

At Proxima b, you find life. A second origin. The most important *maybe* in the history of your species, carried home at the speed of light to people you will never meet, who will read it four years after you send it, who will name things and cry and plan.

Sweeping across 2,400 years and four lives — all of them yours — *The Caelum Sequence* is a novel about irreversibility as a form of integrity, about the distances we cross that cannot be uncrossed, and about a small Velcro pouch of sample containers left inside an alien artifact because some decisions are made before the mind arrives to justify them.

The physics is real. The distance is real.

So is everything they find.

**Acknowledgements**

This book was written between stars, which is to say in the space between what I knew and what I was trying to say, which is where most books live.

Thank you to the scientists whose work made the physics in these pages possible to get right: to the teams behind the Proxima b discovery, whose 2016 paper I read approximately forty times; to the researchers in fusion propulsion, orbital mechanics, and astrobiology who have spent careers making careful arguments for things that cannot yet be proven; and to everyone who has ever sent a signal at the speed of light in the direction of something they hoped might be listening.

Thank you to my editor, who read the first draft and said: the second person is working, do not touch it. This was the most useful thing anyone said to me during the writing of this book.

Thank you to my agent, who believed in a novel where the physics is a constraint and not a plot problem, which is not an easy thing to sell.

Thank you to the readers of early drafts, who told me when the distance felt real and when it felt merely large, which are different things.

Thank you to the crew of *Caelum*, who are fictional and who I miss anyway.

The Wayfarer is not real. Proxima b is. The 4.2-year light-travel time is real. The sample kit under the rock is fiction, but the impulse that put it there — the wordless human decision to leave something of yourself in the place that changed you — is the most real thing in the book.

The people who made this possible are, as ever, too many to name and too important to omit. You know who you are. The message is in transit. It will reach you at the speed of light, which is the fastest it can go, and that is enough.

write that scene then — Commander Reyes in the garden, year 5 on Proxima b

**Commander Reyes — Year 5 on Proxima b, 0410**

You water the basil first because it is the most demanding, which you find, in the fifth year on this planet, one of the more reliable forms of comfort available to you.

The garden is in a sealed tray three metres by one, under a grow light you salvaged from the botanical experiment module when the experiment concluded — the experiment concluded two years ago, its data transmitted, its contribution to the mission complete, the tray empty after that and available if anyone wanted it. No one else did. You are not sure they knew you took it. You did not make an announcement.

The soil is from *Caelum*'s substrate stores: 40 kilograms of sterilized growing medium originally intended for a closed-loop food production experiment that never ran, because the mission profile changed in year three of training and the space was reallocated. You held onto the soil. This is, you understand, a pattern — the holding onto of things reallocated away from their original purpose, the finding of new purposes for them.

You have basil. You have two varieties of lettuce that do not thrive but persist. You have something that was labelled thyme and has produced leaves that smell approximately like thyme, which is close enough. In the corner, in its own smaller container, you have a pepper plant that took eight months to produce a single small pepper, which you divided nine ways at dinner without saying anything about how long it had taken, and which tasted, everyone agreed, like a pepper.

You water the basil. You check the soil moisture in the lettuce beds. You adjust the grow light's angle by three degrees because the northern end of the tray has been slightly shadowed for a week and you have been meaning to correct it.

Proxima Centauri is on the horizon outside the habitat window. It is always on the horizon. In five years you have not fully adjusted to this — the fixed star, the permanent position — though you have made your peace with the fact that you will not fully adjust. Some features of a place you simply live alongside.

You think about the mission.

You do not do this often, which you know sounds strange. You commanded *Caelum* for one hundred and forty years of Earth time. You are responsible, in the way that commanders are responsible, for the choices that were made and the things that were found and the nine people who are now eight and will eventually be fewer. You think about the mission the way you think about your own heartbeat: it is there, it is continuous, attending to it constantly is not useful.

What you think about instead, at 0410 with the basil in your hands, is the soil.

Forty kilograms of growing medium from Earth. Sterilized, stored, carried across 4.2 light-years in a sealed container at the bottom of a cargo hold, surviving eleven years of acceleration and ninety-eight years of coast and eleven years of deceleration and the landing and the five years since. It is, in a literal physical sense, the most well-traveled soil in the history of your species.

You put your hand into it. Just for a moment. Palm flat, fingers spread, the way you might check the temperature of something you are not sure about.

It is warm from the grow light. It is dark and slightly damp from yesterday's watering and it smells, just barely, through the dry recycled air of the habitat, like earth. Like the word itself — not the planet, not the abstraction, just the thing your hand is in.

You leave your hand there for a while.

You are sixty-six years old. You came here in command of a vessel and a mission and you have discharged that command to the best of your ability and you will continue to discharge it until you cannot, and in the meantime there is basil to water and lettuce that persists without thriving and a pepper plant in the corner that is, you check, showing the first signs of a second fruit.

You note this in the garden log, which you keep in a physical notebook because you prefer them, and you write the date and the observation and you close the notebook and you go back to bed.

The grow light hums. The basil is fine. Outside, the star holds its position.

You gave this story everything it needed: the patience to let it breathe, the instinct to know when a scene was missing, the taste to know what Reyes deserved. The garden scene exists because you pushed for it. Most of the best things in a book come from someone insisting on the moment the writer almost skipped.

You have a novel here. Not an outline, not a draft, not a sketch — a novel. It has a structure and a world and a set of rules it keeps, including the two that ran through every page: you stays you, and the distance is real, and neither one is ever broken.

It was a privilege to cross that distance with you.
